Physics Brochure

Kinematics · Forces · Circular Motion

Three Topics at a Glance

What We're Exploring

🚀

Kinematics

The study of motion — describing how objects move through space and time using displacement, velocity, and acceleration.

⚡

Forces

Newton's Laws explain why objects speed up, slow down, or stay still — the push and pull behind all motion in the universe.

🔄

Circular Motion

The physics of objects moving in circles — from spinning rides and orbiting planets to every curve you take in a car.

Topic 1

Kinematics

Kinematics is the branch of physics that describes motion without asking what causes it. It uses four key quantities to tell the full story of any moving object.

  • →Displacement (Δx) — how far and in what direction an object moved from start to finish
  • →Velocity (v) — speed with direction; average velocity = Δx / Δt
  • →Acceleration (a) — rate of change of velocity; a = Δv / Δt
  • →Time (t) — the independent variable tying all quantities together

The Big 4 Equations

v = v₀ + at
x = v₀t + ½at²
v² = v₀² + 2aΔx
x = ½(v + v₀)t

✏️ Kinematics Sample Problem

Kinematics

Scientists & Careers

Key Scientists

🔭

Galileo Galilei (1564–1642)

First measured free-fall acceleration (~9.8 m/s²) using inclined planes

🍎

Isaac Newton (1643–1727)

Unified kinematics into a mathematical framework; invented calculus

✏️

Christiaan Huygens (1629–1695)

Derived equations for circular and pendulum motion

Careers Using Kinematics

Aerospace Engineer

Models rocket trajectories; calculates orbital paths using kinematic equations

Sports Analyst / Biomechanist

Analyzes athlete motion; optimizes speed, angle, and jumping mechanics

Video Game Developer

Programs realistic in-game physics (velocity, gravity, collision) using kinematic models

Topic 2

Forces

Forces are pushes or pulls that change an object's motion. Newton's three laws govern everything from a rolling ball to a rocket launch.

  • Law 1Inertia — an object at rest stays at rest unless a net force acts on it
  • Law 2F = ma — net force equals mass times acceleration
  • Law 3Action-Reaction — for every force there is an equal and opposite force

Common Forces

Gravity (Fg) Fg = mg
Normal Force (N) N = mg (flat)
Friction (Ff) Ff = μN
Net Force ΣF = ma

✏️ Forces Sample Problem

Forces

Scientists & Careers

Key Scientists

🍎

Isaac Newton (1687)

Published Principia Mathematica — the 3 Laws of Motion that define all classical forces

🌌

Robert Hooke (1635–1703)

Discovered Hooke's Law: spring force = k × extension (F = kx)

⚙️

Charles-Augustin de Coulomb (1736–1806)

Measured friction forces precisely; also studied electrostatic forces

Careers Using Forces

Structural / Civil Engineer

Calculates tension, compression, and friction to keep bridges and buildings standing

Mechanical Engineer

Designs machines, engines, and vehicles using Newton's Laws to predict performance

Physical Therapist

Uses biomechanical force analysis to design safe rehab exercises for patients

Topic 3

Circular Motion

Circular motion describes objects moving along a curved or circular path. Even at constant speed, the object is always accelerating because its direction keeps changing — toward the center.

  • →Centripetal Acceleration (aᶜ) — always points toward the center; aᶜ = v²/r
  • →Centripetal Force (Fᶜ) — the net inward force; Fᶜ = mv²/r
  • →Period (T) — time to complete one full revolution; T = 2πr/v
  • →Tangential Speed (v) — how fast along the circle; v = 2πr/T

Key Equations

aᶜ = v² / r
Fᶜ = mv² / r
T = 2πr / v
v = 2πr / T

✏️ Circular Motion Sample Problem

Circular Motion

Scientists & Careers

Key Scientists

🍎

Isaac Newton (1687)

Derived the formula for centripetal force and showed gravity keeps the Moon in orbit

🔭

Christiaan Huygens (1659)

First derived the equation aᶜ = v²/r for centripetal acceleration mathematically

🌍

Johannes Kepler (1609)

Discovered the 3 laws of planetary orbital motion — the original circular motion in nature

Careers Using Circular Motion

Aerospace / Orbital Engineer

Uses Fᶜ = mv²/r to calculate the exact speed needed to keep satellites in stable orbit

Mechanical / Automotive Engineer

Designs banked roads and race tracks so cars can safely corner at high speeds

Roller Coaster / Ride Designer

Calculates minimum loop speed so riders feel safe g-forces at the top of a loop

At a Glance

Topic Comparison

Math Difficulty (1–5 scale)

🚀 Kinematics 3 / 5
⚡ Forces 2 / 5
🔄 Circular Motion 3 / 5

Real-World Relevance

🚀Kinematics → Self-driving cars, rockets, sports analytics
⚡Forces → Architecture, sports medicine, vehicle safety
🔄Circular Motion → Satellites, roller coasters, race tracks

Key Facts Worth Knowing

Kinematics

Free fall acceleration = 9.8 m/s² (gravity, no air resistance)

Forces

Newton published Principia in 1687 — still valid 337 years later

Circular Motion

The Moon orbits Earth at ~1,022 m/s — perfectly balanced centripetal force

Everyday Connection

All three topics apply to something you do every day — driving, walking, or listening to music
